dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain at a residence in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Alex from the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I was trying to fall asleep when I began to have chest pain, along with nausea and dizziness. It felt like something heavy was on my chest.
Mr. Robert, can you tell me your name and the date today?
My name is Robert, and today is December 17th.
What city are we in, and can you tell me your age?
We're in [CITY], and I am 92 years old. I can do several arm movements for you if needed.
Mr. Robert is alert and oriented to person, place, and time with a GCS of 15.
Your breathing appears unlabored at the moment.
Pulse is strong and regular. Skin appears perfused with no cyanosis noted.
Respirations are 15 per minute right now.
Blood pressure is 218 over 85 and pulse is 80 beats per minute.
Have you experienced any trouble or strain in your breathing at any point tonight?
Have you felt any discomfort or tenderness in your stomach since the episode began?
Any sharp or dull pain in your back?
Can you describe the sensation and location of the chest pain more clearly?
The pain started as a tight sensation on the left side of my chest. It felt like my chest was compressed, like something was pressing on it. After a time, the discomfort improved.
Was the sensation tightness, pressure, or something different?
Most definitely on the left side. There was no spreading to the left arm or jaw.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe did your chest discomfort become at the worst?
It lasted about 30 minutes. I now feel significantly better, although I still have a touch of dizziness.
Are there any medications or substances you are allergic to?
I don't have known allergies to any medications.
Can you tell me which medications you take on a regular basis?
I currently take Tylenol, Vytorin, Tessalon, Xalatan, and Advair. I have not taken any other medications tonight.
Any known medical history that might be relevant to this event?
Yes, I've had a past heart attack and I suffer from high blood pressure. Also, I've had GI bleed issues and high cholesterol.
When was the last time you had something to eat or drink, Mr. Robert?
I had chicken soup approximately 30 minutes ago before the episode started.
Pulse is 80 and regular.
Respirations remain at 15 per minute.
Blood pressure is 218 over 85 mmHg.
Glucose is 131 mg/dL currently.
SpO₂ is at 91%.
EKG shows a sinus rhythm with premature ventricular contractions.
I will now apply a four-lead ECG for ongoing monitoring of your heart activity.
Protocol 2-1 is appropriately initiated based on your elevated blood pressure and possible cardiac involvement.
I will perform a 12-lead EKG to get more detailed cardiac information. Please stay as still as possible.
The 12-lead reflects infrequent PVCs, and no new cardiac activity from the initial assessment.
I am now securing a 20-gauge IV line on your left arm for possible future interventions. I only needed one attempt for successful placement.
Pulse is 74 now, slightly lower than before.
Your breathing is now 16 per minute and remains unlabored.
Blood pressure is now 231 over 80 mmHg.
SpO₂ has improved to 95%.
Repeat EKG shows the same as before-PVCs with regular sinus rhythm.
Has there been any change in the nature or intensity of your discomfort since we first spoke?
Can you confirm the pattern and where the discomfort was most intense?
It was on the left, now mostly gone. I just feel faint and tired.
Our protocol remains 2-1 for safety, but your condition appears stable at this point.
Mr. Robert, where would you prefer us to take you for further medical evaluation and care?
Please transport me to [COMPANY_NAME]. I'd prefer to be treated there if possible.
Mr. Robert, we will now transfer you from the bed to the stretcher with care.
We are using the stand and pivot method to secure you onto the stretcher. Please hold the railings for support.
You are now physically secured onto the stretcher. The straps and side rails are in place for safety.
We are ready for transport. Please tell me if you feel anything uncomfortable.
No new findings; vitals remain stable.
Can you rate your current discomfort again now that we are en route?
The chest pain now feels like a 3. I'm mainly just hazy these days.
IV access is patent. Site shows no signs of discomfort or infiltration.
Your pulse remains at 74 and SpO₂ at 95%. Blood pressure reads 231/80.
You appear calm with no indications of distress at the moment.
Can you describe your activity just before the symptoms began?
I was lying down in bed attempting to sleep when the discomfort started. It resolved on its own shortly after.
Your skin has no visible lesions or rashes.
Head shows no deformity or signs of trauma.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth, and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No nasal obstruction or trauma. Hearing appears normal.
No visible chest asymmetry or signs of deformity.
Your abdomen is non-tender. I can confirm no guarding.
No deformities or angulations in extremities. No signs of restricted movement.
IV access is in working condition.
Vital signs remain consistent with prior evaluations.
Do you think the chest discomfort will resolve as quickly if it occurs again?
It usually does, but not always. I hope it doesn't come back.
[CITY], we have a 92-year-old male with resolved chest discomfort. No active cardiac indication at this time.
Patient is stable per protocol 2-1. No ASA administered due to GI bleed history.
Our planned destination for Mr. Robert is [COMPANY_NAME] as requested.
Patient remains asymptomatic of distress and comfortable throughout transport.
ETA is currently approximately 10 minutes. No changes in condition during transit.
[CITY], the patient is secured and ready for handoff.
Vitals stable, responsive and asymptomatic of active distress. All actions and documentation are complete.
Rechecking blood pressure: 231/80. Pulse remains 74, SpO₂ is 95%.
IV remains clear with no observed complications.
Mr. Robert stays quiet and relaxed, no signs of distress.
No changes in condition during transport; patient emplaced in [CITY] care protocol.
Mr. Robert, you are now being transferred to the ED for further evaluation. All interventions documented.
